coredata.dc:title: Improved mechanical-physical performances of copper composites with reduced graphene oxide and carbonized polymer dots as multi-scale synergetic reinforcements
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ruofei
He, Yan
Liu, Liang
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Tao, Jingmei
Li, Caiju
Mu, Xiaonan
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance (structure-function integration) Cu matrix composites have become a focus by introducing the novel hybrid carbon nanomaterials and giving full play to the synergistic effect. It is a great challenge to homogeneously disperse the ex-situ nano-filler within matrix for excellent performances. In this study, the two-dimensional reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots (CPD) were incorporated with Cu-based powders by the molecular level mixing (MLM) and high-energy ball milling (HBM) methods, respectively. Results revealed that RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu bulk composites sintered at 650 °C displayed higher ultimate tensile strength (367 MPa) and better failure strain (∼30%) as compared with pure Cu (MLM/HBM). Meanwhile, the electrical conductivity of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu was almost equal to that of pure Cu (93.74% IACS), exhibiting a promising prospect in applications as advanced multifunctional structural materials. It was well proved that the interface strength of RGO-Cu and the dispersibility of hybrid reinforcements were improved by the incorporation of CPD, which further enhanced the synergistic effects of (CPD + RGO) and achieved better mechanical-physical performances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu matrix composite
Carbonized polymer dots (CPD)
Reduced graphene oxide (RGO)
Synergistic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Raw materials and synthetized composite powders
Comparisons of the single and synergistic reinforcements

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52174345 , 52064032 ) and the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (Grant No. 202002AB080001 ). Science and Technology Major Project of Yunnan Province (Grant No. 202202AG050004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process: (a) RGO-Cu (MLM) composite powder, (b) CPD-Cu (HBM) composite powder, and (c) RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu bulk composites.
TEM images of (a) as-received GO; (b–c) morphology feature and HRTEM image of CPD synthesized by hydrothermal reaction, (d) higher magnified inverse FFT pattern of CPD.
XPS survey spectrum (a) and high-resolution spectra of C1s (b), O1s (c) and N1s (d) of CPD.

SEM images of (a–b) RGO-Cu (MLM) composite powder after reduction; (c) morphology of RGO-Cu (HBM) composite powder fabricated by high-energy ball milling, the inset shows RGO is distributed on the surface of the Cu flakes; (d) as-received dendritic Cu powder; (e) CPD-Cu (HBM) composite powder fabricated by high-energy ball milling; (f) RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite powder obtained by simply mixing process.
XRD patterns of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites sintered at different temperatures.

The relative density, electrical conductivity and tensile mechanical properties of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites sintered at different temperatures: (a) the trend chart of relative density and conductivity; (b) tensile engineering stress-strain curves; (c) the trend chart of hardness, ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and elongation with the variation of sintering temperatures; (d) true stress-strain curves; (e) work hardening rate curves calculated from (d).
Fractured morphologies of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites sintered at different temperatures: (a) 600 °C; (b–c) 650 °C, (d) 700 °C, (e) 750 °C, and (f) 800 °C.

Electrical and mechanical properties of pure Cu and composites with single or two reinforcements: (a) relative density and electrical conductivity; (b) tensile engineering stress-strain curves; (c) work hardening rate-true strain curves; the variation tendency of tensile engineering stress (UTS) as a function of (d) electrical conductivity and (e) fractured elongation; (f) comparison of strength versus elongation of composite with references [7,11,12,16,21,24–33].

Interfacial microstructure between RGO and Cu in RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite: (a–b) TEM im ages in bright-field and dark-field modes; (c) HRTEM image of the microstructure interface between RGO and Cu matrix; (d–e) FFT and IFFT i mages of the marked boxes in (c); (f) TEM/EDX mapping images of grain boundary area in RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite.

Interfacial microstructure between CPD and Cu matrix in RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite: (a) bright-field TEM image of CPD clusters distributed within Cu matrix, (b) HRTEM image of the CPD-Cu interface; (c–e) FFT and IFFT images of regions c, d, and e.

(a–d) TEM images show CPD distributes on the RGO surface (CPD@RGO) by different magnification in RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite, (c–d) FFT pattern and IFFT images of CPD in the marked box in (b), (e–f) the morphologies of twins' crystals in RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite.
Comparisons of the UV–vis spectra of Cu (MLM/HBM) and RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites.
Performances statistics of pure Cu and composites with single or two reinforcements.
The elements content of Area I calculating by TEM/EDX mapping.

head.ce:title: Improved mechanical-physical performances of copper composites with reduced graphene oxide and carbonized polymer dots as multi-scale synergetic reinforcements

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance (structure-function integration) Cu matrix composites have become a focus by introducing the novel hybrid carbon nanomaterials and giving full play to the synergistic effect. It is a great challenge to homogeneously disperse the ex-situ nano-filler within matrix for excellent performances. In this study, the two-dimensional reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots (CPD) were incorporated with Cu-based powders by the molecular level mixing (MLM) and high-energy ball milling (HBM) methods, respectively. Results revealed that RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu bulk composites sintered at 650 °C displayed higher ultimate tensile strength (367 MPa) and better failure strain (∼30%) as compared with pure Cu (MLM/HBM). Meanwhile, the electrical conductivity of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu was almost equal to that of pure Cu (93.74% IACS), exhibiting a promising prospect in applications as advanced multifunctional structural materials. It was well proved that the interface strength of RGO-Cu and the dispersibility of hybrid reinforcements were improved by the incorporation of CPD, which further enhanced the synergistic effects of (CPD + RGO) and achieved better mechanical-physical performances.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Although copper has good ductility and excellent electrical and thermal properties, its slightly lower mechanical strength undoubtedly restricts its further use for structural components [1,2]. Thus, there is an imperative need to develop copper matrix composites with superior structure-function integration performances. Due to their exceptional inherent properties, carbon nanomaterials (CNMs), such as carbonized polymer dots (CPD), one-dimensional carbon nanotubes (CNTs), two-dimensional graphene, and their derivatives, are regarded as the ideal reinforcing nano-fillers for metal-based composites [3–6]. Previous studies have found that incorporating CNMs would lead to an increase in strength for Cu materials, but this comes at the expense of plasticity, electrical conductivity, or other physical properties [7,8]. The bottlenecks mainly consist of three aspects: 1) the existence of van der Waals forces between CNMs and the specific gravity difference between CNMs and Cu matrix may lead to agglomeration; 2) the weak wettability between CNMs and Cu matrix makes it difficult to form a valid interface bonding; and 3) the structural integrity of CNMs is destroyed during special preparation route. In an effort to address these issues, Yang et al. [9] prepared GNRs/Cu composites with well-balanced mechanical-physical properties by unzipping CNTs into graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) for reinforcing pure Cu. The novel quasi-one-dimensional CNMs combine elegantly the properties of graphene and CNTs to provide insight into the viability of regaining good plasticity and conductivity that defied the boundaries of traditional composites.

The synergistic enhancement effect is the introduction of multiple components or multi-scale reinforcing phases into the metal matrix using the characteristics and spatial distribution of the various reinforcements in order to fully exploit their individual merits and make up for other shortcomings [10–12]. For instance, Zhang et al. [10] proposed a novel in situ space-confined strategy to construct a well-dispersed CNT embedded in a three-dimensional graphene network (3D GN) hybrid structure and fabricated 3D GN/CNT hybrids reinforced Cu composites. The 3D CNT-GN/Cu bulk composites exhibited a tensile strength of 343 MPa and a failure strain of 15%. Thus, 3D GN/CNT hybrid-reinforcement exhibited a significant strengthening efficiency and a more balanced strength-ductility compatibility, as compared with a single component (CNT or 3D GN) at the same content. Chen et al. [13] investigated the interface interaction between functionalized nano-fillers and the Cu matrix. The trade-off between Orowan strengthening and load-transfer effects in CNMs/Cu composites was discussed to understand the synergistic strengthening behavior of CNT-RGO hybrids. This basic theoretical research may provide new insights into the synergistic strengthening effects of Cu matrix composites reinforced with hybridization of reinforcements.

Very recently, CPD has been employed as a novel carbon reinforcement in copper matrix. CPD has a polymer/carbon hybrid structure rather than the structure of a carbon main body. It has an essentially spherical shape with a diameter of less than 10 nm [14,15]. The core is a carbon nanoparticle with a sp2 hybrid, and the surface has a variety of oxygen-containing functional groups, which gives it better dispersibility in copper salt solutions as a colloid compared with graphene and CNTs [12,16]. Zhao et al. [17] fabricated 0.2 wt% CPD/Cu composites by the powder metallurgy route and the as-sintered bulk composites displayed high electrical conductivity (98% IACS) and enhanced tensile strength without an obvious decrease in elongation. Similarly, Liu et al. [18] demonstrated that CPD/Cu composites had simultaneously improved tensile strength and elongation in comparison with graphene/Cu composites. The improvement in mechanical properties was correlated to the significantly improved interface bonding due to the N-doping. However, the relationship between the interfacial structure and mechanical properties is still poorly understood, and the synergistic reinforcement mechanism of CPD with other CNMs is still up for debate [19,20].

In this study, the typical zero-dimensional CPDs and two-dimensional reduced graphene oxide (RGO) were selected as the hybrid nano-fillers. The numerous functional groups on their surface aid in the interfacial bonding between the copper and the reinforcements. CPD and RGO were mixed with Cu-based powders using the molecular-level mixing method (MLM) and high-energy ball milling method (HBM), respectively, to better realize their dispersibility in the matrix. The effects of consolidation temperature on the composite performances, as well as the synergistic effect of CPD and RGO were systematically investigated and discussed.

The as-received graphene oxide (GO) powders was fabricated by the modified Hummers' method and the obtained powders was sonicated in deionized water for 30 min to form the uniform suspension. After that, GO suspension was gradually added to the copper acetate monohydrate (0.0156 M) solution and magnetic stirring continuously in a water bath at 75 °C. Then, 4 M NaOH solution (50 mL) was added to the mixture, and 2 M anhydrous glucose solution (20 mL) was subsequently added. After that, GO was incorporated with Cu2O particles by the MLM method, and the synthesized composite powder (abbreviated as: GO-Cu2O (MLM)) was obtained after washing, suction filtration, and drying. The RGO-Cu (MLM) composite powder was obtained by a thorough reduction (400 °C for 300 min) using nitrogen-hydrogen mixed gas. By means of the O/N/H analysis meter (HORIBA EMGA-830, Japan) to measure the impurity elements of the RGO-Cu (MLM) composite powder, the results shows that the contents of N, O and H are 0.0004%, 0.11% and ∼0% in atomic percent (at.%), respectively. The calculated volume fraction of RGO was 1.5% after measuring by the high-precision carbon sulfur analyzer (Suzhou Sky-ray Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The RGO-Cu (MLM) powder preparation process is schematically depicted in Fig. 1 (a).

Fig. 1(b) shows the fabrication process of CPD-Cu composite powder via the high-energy ball milling method (abbreviated as CPD-Cu (HBM)). Initially, ethylenediamine and citric acid were combined in a hydrothermal reaction at 150 °C for 360 min to produce CPD powder. The synthetic method was described in detail in our previous papers [11,12,16]. After that, 0.06 g of synthesized CPD powder was dispersed in anhydrous alcohol. Then, the electrolytic pure copper-based powder (99.9% purity, 325 mesh particle size, Shanghai Naiou Nano Science and Technology Co. Ltd., China) and CPD solution were mixed by high-energy ball milling at a speed of 280 rpm for 10 h with a ball-to-powder ratio of 10:1. The CPD-Cu (HBM) composite powder was created after filtering and drying, and the volume fraction of CPD was estimated to be 1.5% vol.

The above-fabricated RGO-Cu (MLM) and CPD-Cu (HBM) composite powders were mixed at a volume ratio of 1:3 to obtain an RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite powder. Afterward, these powders were consolidated at various target temperatures (600 °C, 650 °C, 700 °C, 750 °C, and 800 °C) by spark plasma sintering (SPS) with an applied pressure of 50 MPa, as shown in Fig. 1(c). During the SPS process, the working chamber was vacuumized firstly until the environmental pressure was lower than 2.0 × 10−4 Pa and then triggered the heating stage. For comparison, pure copper monolithic (Cu (MLM), Cu (HBM), and Cu (MLM/HBM)) were prepared under the same conditions. Meanwhile, RGO (MLM)-Cu, RGO (HBM)-Cu, CPD (MLM)-Cu, CPD (HBM)-Cu, and RGO (HBM)/CPD (MLM)-Cu bulk composites containing the same volume fraction of reinforcement were also prepared.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA). According to the suggestions of L. Hultman [44], in order to gain more accurate data, the analysis was taken out under the following conditions. The base pressure during spectra acquisition is lower than 3*10−7 mbar. The excitation source is monochromatic Al Kα radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV). The working voltage is 12 kV, the filament current is 6 mA and the size of analyzed area is 400 μm. The electron emission angle is 45°. For all measurements on CPD powder sample, the charge neutralizer is used [45–47]. UPS method was employed to measure the work function (ΦSA) of as-synthesized CPD sample by Thermo Scientific NEXSA(USA) in order to confirm the calibration of energy bonding (EB), which is related to the work function (ΦSA). The analysis parameters are as follow: the base pressure during spectra acquisition is lower than 5*10−9 mbar. The excitation source is monochromatic Al Kα radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV). The working voltage is 15 kV, the filament current is 15 mA, the analyzer mode is CAE (pass energy: 2.0 eV). We measured ΦSA through Thermo Avantage. We first imported the measured Ag standard sample data and transform the x-axis from binding energy to kinetic energy, then input the bias voltage (−5) we had during the test. After that, the cut-off energy (8.96 eV), Fermi level KE (25.91 eV) and the work function (4.27 eV) were recorded. The sample data were imported, according to the bias voltage and the Fermi level of Ag standard sample, the work function of as-synthesized CPD sample was calculated directly, which is 4.49 eV. According to the equation: EB = 289.58-ΦSA [45,46], the calculated EB is 285.09 eV.

The morphology and microstructure of the composite powders and sintered bulks were researched by the field emission electron microscope (FESEM, Nano Nova45) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 TF30 S-Twin). The phase composition and crystallinity degree of the bulk composites were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, RINT, Rigaku). The hardness, conductivity, and relative density of the bulk composites were measured using the Vickers hardness tester (DHV1000CCD), the portable digital eddy current conductivity meter (Sigma 2008B), and the fully automatic electronic density meter (FX-300Y). Uniaxial tensile tests were performed on the specimens with a gauge cross-section of 2 mm × 1.2 mm in an AG-X-100 kN machine at a nominal strain rate of 5 10−4 s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raw materials and synthetized composite powders
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (a) displays the morphology of as-received GO. The lamellar thickness is ∼10 nm with abundant wrinkles on the surface. The laboratory-produced CPD has spherical and ellipsoidal morphologies, exhibiting good dispersion in the alcohol, as shown in Fig. 2(b). According to the statistics on particle size, it has an average particle size of about 2.28 nm, with a size distribution range of 0.30–4.52 nm (at least 300 particles were counted in total). The clear lattice fringe of CPD can be seen in the high resolution TEM image in Fig. 2(c). The fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern in Fig. 2(c) inset indicates a typical graphite phase with the (111) and (200) crystal planes. By analyzing the higher magnified inverse FFT pattern in Fig. 2(c), the inter-planar spacing of these lattice fringes in Fig. 2(d) is 0.207 nm and 0.189 nm, which correspond to the (111) and (200) planes of CPD, respectively.

In order to gain more accurate chemical binding information from XPS analysis, the high-resolution spectra was performed by Thermo Avantage and fitted by Gaussian Lorentzian functions. As can be judged from Fig. 3 , the fit quality is very good, the fitted curve (shown in red) closely follows the raw data (shown in line with symbol). Fig. 3(a) shows the XPS full spectrum of CPD presented three representative peaks at 286 eV, 401 eV and 531 eV, which corresponds to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 3(b)) displays three peaks at 288.60 eV, 286.55 eV, 285.07 eV and 285.04 eV, corresponding to OC–O, C–O/C–N and C–C/CC. The high-resolution N 1s spectra of CPD (Fig. 3(d)) shows three peaks at 401.59 eV, 399.86 eV and 398.75 eV, which are attributed to graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N respectively. The O 1s spectra of CPD in Fig. 3(c) exhibits three peaks at 535.5 eV, 532.73 eV and 531.31 eV, which assigned to OC–O, C–OH and C–O–C.

The SEM image in Fig. 4 (a) shows the morphology of the RGO-Cu (MLM) composite powder after reduction. The synthetic RGO-Cu (MLM) particles have regular cube-like shapes and a uniform size distribution (mean particle size of ∼1.75 μm). It is important to note that the reduced Cu particles exhibit a mass of RGO embedded within them, and that the edges and surfaces of the particles are noticeably rough at higher magnification. Although the copper-graphene system generally exhibits poor wettability, it illustrates the robust interface bonding between RGO and Cu-based particles. During the thermal reduction of Cu2O to Cu element, oxygen atoms would be diffused from the Cu2O interior and escaped, therefore, leaving a large number of channels for the diffusion of oxygen atoms on the powder surface, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The RGO-Cu (HBM) composite powder in Fig. 4(c), in contrast to the conventional dispersion method, which introduces RGO by high-energy ball milling (HBM), exhibits weak interface adhesion. RGO is simply attached to the surface of Cu flake powder. Fig. 4(e) shows the morphology of ball milled CPD-Cu (HBM) composite powder. CPD tends to diffuse or hide in the numerous “surface pits” of Cu-based powders due to the obvious surface roughness in the raw dendritic Cu powder (see Fig. 4(d)). The rapid flattening of electrolytic Cu powder is therefore expected to encapsulate CPD within the Cu flakes following the milling procedure. As a result, it is challenging to see where CPD is located on the Cu flake surface in the ball-milled CPD-Cu (HBM) powder. Fig. 4(f) shows that RGO-Cu (MLM) particles are uniformly distributed on the lamellar surface of CPD-Cu (HBM) powder, which is similar to how “sesame seeds attach to biscuits”. On the other hand, it is certain that the incorporation of RGO brought about by MLM can guarantee the dispersion properties and prevent any potential secondary agglomeration of RGO.

Fig. 5 displays the results of an XRD analysis of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites sintered at various temperatures. The diffraction peaks correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes of Cu. However, due to its tiny content and lower Bragg diffraction intensity, the diffraction intensity of carbon was difficult to observe.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the variations of relative density and conductivity of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu bulk composites at different sintering temperatures. It is clear that these composites, which were sintered at 600 °C–800 °C, have relative densities that are higher than 98%. Additionally, these composites have electrical conductivities that are higher than 93.5% and that are not greatly affected by temperature changes. Fig. 6(b) presents the tensile engineering stress-strain curves of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites (sintered at 600 °C–800 °C), and the variation trends including hardness, ultimate tensile strength (UTS), and fracture elongation are illustrated in Fig. 6(c). It is discovered that the hardness and UTS of these composites increase and then decrease as the sintering temperature rises, reaching their maximum values of 113 HV and 367 MPa at 650 °C, respectively. The elongation, follows a different trajectory. Achieving a value of 33.2% at 650 °C and a maximum value of 46.5% at 800 °C, it first decreases and then increases in response to the sintering temperature. The RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite still achieves excellent strength-plasticity compatibility when sintered at 650 °C, despite the fact that the UTS and elongation have an opposite trend correlated with temperature. For a clearer understanding, Table S1 in the Supplementary Material lists the specific tendency for variation in the electrical and mechanical properties of pure Cu (MLM/HBM) and RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites sintered at various temperatures.

The true stress-strain curves and strain hardening rate curves of these composites, which were obtained from various sintering temperatures, are shown in Fig. 6(d and e). The maximum UTS of 450 MPa has been reached by the true stress of the RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite (sintered at 650 °C). According to the strain-hardening rate curves, the composites sintered at 800°Chave reached the end of the linear hardening stage and have begun the uniform deformation at a true strain of 2.5%, while those sintered at 600 °C, 700 °C, and 750 °C have reached a true strain of 3%. The uniform plastic deformation has only begun for the composites sintered at 650 °C with a true strain of 5%. Evidently, the composite sintered at 650 °C experiences more linear hardening stage deformation and attains a higher work hardening rate. Hence, the deformation resistance of the composite is further enhanced. During plastic deformation, the increase in dislocation density and the resultant pinning effect are decisive factors for work hardening. Therefore, it can be speculated that the RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite sintered at 650 °C would produce more dislocation interactions during the tensile tests. However, work hardening has also brought a decrease in plasticity, which corresponds to the fracture that first occurs at 650 °C.

In Fig. 7 (a–f), the fractured morphologies of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites sintered at 600°C–800 °C are depicted. All of the composites exhibit typical ductile fracture characteristics, and there are many dimples on the fracture surfaces. The fact that the outermost dimples in Fig. 7(c) are cracked RGO fragments shows that the RGO is uniformly dispersed in the Cu matrix and forms a strong interface bond with the Cu matrix, allowing for a high load transfer contribution and enhancing tensile strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparisons of the single and synergistic reinforcements
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8(a–c) and (g) illustrate the bar charts of the relative density and electrical conductivity, engineering stress-strain curves, and strain hardening rate curves of pure Cu and composites (sintered at 650 °C). The specific values of the relative density, electrical properties, and mechanical properties of all bulks are recorded in Table 1 .

The highest relative density (99.06%) and electrical conductivity (96.73% IACS) are found in pure Cu (HBM) processed by high-energy ball milling, as shown in Fig. 8(a–b). Even though pure Cu (MLM) that has undergone molecular-level mixing processing is stronger than pure Cu (HBM), the elongation is not satisfactory. The tensile strength of Cu (MLM/HBM) is 287 MPa, which is higher than Cu (MLM) and Cu (HBM), and maintains an acceptable elongation of 32.7%. Furthermore, the relative density of composites incorporated with a single reinforcement is 98% (in Fig. 8(a)). It should be noted that when compared to RGO-Cu, CPD-Cu composites have higher electrical conductivity and elongation. The electrical properties and plasticity of CPD-Cu composites prepared by the HBM are superior to those prepared by the MLM when the reinforcements are the same but the processes are different. In brief, it is confirmed that the HBM method is more suitable for fabricating the CPD-Cu composites.

RGO (HBM)/CPD (MLM)-Cu and RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites have a relative density of more than 98% and a higher UTS than a single reinforcement. Particularly, the UTS and elongation of the RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite are 367 MPa and 33.2%, respectively, and are higher than those of the RGO (HBM)/CPD (MLM)-Cu and Cu (MLM/HBM). As seen in Fig. 8(c), RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu still exhibits the longest linear hardening in comparison to other materials. It implies that the CPD introduced by the HBM method is beneficial to the elongation during uniform plastic deformation. The diagrams of UTS as a function of electrical conductivity and failure elongation for pure Cu and RGO/CPD-Cu systems are shown in Fig. 8(d–e). It is demonstrated that the RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite achieves an optimal balance between conductivity-tensile strength-elongation, which displays improved mechanical-physical performances by the multi-scale synergetic reinforcements. The comparison of the RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite in the current study and pertinent works in other studies is shown in Fig. 8(f) [7,11,12,16,21,24–33]. The RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites can achieve an overall improvement in their electrical and mechanical properties thanks to the synergistic effects of RGO and CPD.

The higher electrical conductivity and superior mechanical properties can be better understood by having a thorough understanding of the interfacial microstructure. Fig. 9 (a–b) show the interfacial microstructure of the RGO(MLM)/CPD(HBM)-Cu composite in the bright-field and dark-field modes of the same region, respectively. The serpentine distribution along the grain boundaries is RGO. Meanwhile, the nanoscale particles which are dispersed within the Cu grain interior and at the grain boundaries should be CPD clusters (marked by yellow arrows). During SPS, recrystallization and grains growth of Cu matrix will occur spontaneously, CPDs can be small enough to be located inside matrix grain interiors. On the other hand, sectional of CPDs can also serve as the nucleation sites for recrystallized Cu grains. Thus, most CPD particles/clusters are homogeneously dispersed within the grain interior. No pores were discovered, and the tight interface bonding between the RGO and matrix is clearly visible in the HRTEM image in Fig. 9(c). As shown in Fig. 9(d–e), the FFT image and IFFT image of region-d and e underwent extensive analysis. It is discovered that the region containing the interface has three pairs of diffraction spots corresponding to (1
1 ‾
1 ‾
), (002), and (1
1 ‾
1) crystal planes of Cu2O, which indicates that a transition interface of Cu2O is formed between RGO and the Cu matrix. It means that the RGO and Cu are bonded simultaneously through physical adhesion and chemical “hook pin” via in-situ formed Cu2O.

In the meantime, an interesting phenomenon was also discovered from the TEM/EDX mapping analysis that O element segregation still happened at grain boundaries. Although O element has very low contents (0.43% in atomic percent) according to the O/N/H analysis, the O element content at the grain boundary is higher (1.05% in atomic percent), as shown in Fig. 9(f) and Table 2 . It is believed that the diffusion of O element from CPD particles/clusters happened during the SPS processing, which leads to O element segregation partly at grain boundary.

As shown in Fig. 10 (a), some circular particles with a large size (20–50 nm) are evenly distributed in the composite. It is believed that it results from the original CPD's polymerization reaction at high temperatures during the sintering process, forming CPD clusters. The CPD clusters are shown in Fig. 10(b) in a magnified view. The CPD cluster is surrounded by a shell-like interface that is darker in color, and the round substrate contains a number of circular nanoparticles (ranging in size from 2.0 to 5.0 nm) with distinct lattice stripes dispersed throughout. Three regions in Fig. 10(b) are transformed into FFT and IFFT images, which are shown in Fig. 10(c–e) and (c1-e1), respectively. Firstly, region-c, which corresponds to the Cu (111) crystalline plane, is the outer region of the CPD cluster with a crystalline spacing of 0.206 nm. Secondly, the region-d includes the previously mentioned circular particles and a portion of the shell. The crystal plane spacing for them is measured to be 0.217 nm for Cu2O (200) and 0.209 nm for C (111), respectively. As a result, there is also a transition interface of Cu2O between the CPD cluster and the Cu matrix, and the CPD particles are discernible within the CPD cluster. Region-e contains more internal regions of the CPD cluster. A number of diffraction rings can be seen in addition to the diffraction spots of copper, carbon, and copper dioxide, which shows that the CPD clusters also contain amorphous carbon with poor crystallinity. These CPD particles and amorphous carbon therefore correspond to CPD clusters, which are made up of several CPD particles connected by their surface polymer chains. Specifically, the CPD clusters with amorphous carbon networks serving as the connecting lines and carbon cores serving as the dots.

Notably, a large number of nanoparticles are observed to be uniformly distributed on the partial RGO surface (CPD@RGO), as shown in Fig. 11 (a–b). FFT and IFFT are performed on one of the nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 11(c–d). The three pairs of spots can be seen to correspond to the hexagonal (10–11), (01-1-2), and (1–103) crystalline planes of carbon with spacings of 0.212 nm, 0.208 nm, and 0.203 nm, respectively. This suggests that CPD particles are present in large quantities on the RGO surface. Both CPD and RGO contain sp2 carbon, and there is a possibility of forming π-π stacking between them [34]. At the same time, a large number of twin crystals within the Cu grains are observed in Fig. 11(e–f).

According to the UV–Vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 12 , certain of the absorption peaks of the RGO (HBM)/CPD (MLM)-Cu composite are bathochromically displaced to variable degrees when compared to Cu (MLM/HBM) [35,36]. This suggests that RGO and CPD exhibit π-π interactions with each other. The covalent bond strengths of the π-π interactions are equivalent to those of covalent bonds, making them a powerful interaction force [37]. The CPD particles contribute to the strong interface adhesion by increasing the surface roughness of the RGO and creating a “bridge” to link carbon nano-materials with the Cu matrix.

The experimental results indicate that the simultaneous introduction of RGO and CPD does have a synergistic effect on both electrical and mechanical characteristics. For MMCs reinforced with carbon nanomaterials, there are three common strengthening mechanisms: fine-grained strengthening, dislocation strengthening, and load transfer strengthening [12]. The above three components are added together to provide the reinforcing contribution, which is represented by Eq. (1):
(1)
σ
Y C
=
σ M
+
Δ σ
G R
+
Δ σ
D
+
Δ σ
L T
where
σ
Y C
and σ
M are correspond to the yield strength of RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites and pure Cu (MLM/HBM), respectively. Δσ
GR , Δσ
D , and Δσ
LT are correspond to the contribution of grain refinement, dislocation strengthening, and load transfer strengthening to the composites, respectively.

The major cause of grain refinement in the composites is attributed to RGO and CPD, which restrict grain growth by impeding the movement of grain boundaries during sintering. RGO and CPD anchor the dislocations during the deformation process, which generates an accumulation of dislocations at the interface region and intensifies the dislocations. Twinned grain boundaries have the same effect as high-angle grain boundaries that they are able to effectively stop dislocations from moving and store them.

The load transfer from the matrix to RGO played an important role in strengthening Cu matrix. It is initially assumed that RGO would experience a fracture failure process since the predicted critical diameter (Lc = 0.25 μm) is significantly less than the diameter of RGO employed in this experiment. Thus, based on the fracture failure mechanism of RGO, the Δσ LT contribution was determined [24–26]. The calculated values from fine-grain strengthening, dislocation strengthening, and load transfer strengthening are 18.2 MPa, 22.6 MPa, and 50.9 MPa, respectively. It reveals that the primary strengthening mechanism for increasing strength is still load transfer strengthening (the specific calculation procedure is recorded in the Supplementary Information).

Load transfer is typically determined by the strength of the interface bonding between the reinforcement and matrix. Since RGO is the primary source of load transfer, it is necessary to determine whether the addition of CPD improves the interface bonding by calculating the interface strength (
τ
i s
) between RGO and Cu matrix, as shown by Ref. [38]:
(2)
σ
y c
=
1 2
τ
i s
p
V f
+
σ
y m
(
1 −
V f
)
(
p ＜
p c
)
(3)
σ
y c
=
(
1 −
σ
R G O
2
τ
i s
p
)
σ
R G O
V f
+
σ
y m
(
1 −
V f
)
(
p ＞
p c
)
where
V f
is the volume fraction of RGO (1.5 vol% for RGO (MLM)/Cu composite and 0.75 vol% for RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite),
σ
R G O
is the fracture strength of RGO (30 GPa [39]), p is the aspect ratio of RGO (∼700), p c is the critical aspect ratio of RGO, defined as
p c
=
σ
R G O
τ m
[40],
τ m
the yield shear strength of Cu matrix that is equal to half of matrix yield strength (0.5
σ
y m
= 58 MPa) [41]. For the present composite, p c can be estimated to be 2750, which is much larger than the actual p of RGO (∼700). Hence, the shear lag model should follow Eq. (2) to back estimate the
τ
i s
of the composites.

Accordingly, the obtained
τ
i s
for RGO (MLM)/Cu composite and RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite are 8.96 MPa, and 40.58 MPa, respectively. It has been shown that the introduction of CPD improves the strength of the binding between RGO and Cu. This is related to the strong chemical bonds between (RGO + CPD)-Cu and the π-π stacking that also takes place between RGO and CPD. The advantages of multi-scale synergetic reinforcements include stronger interface bonding, which is necessary to increase load transfer efficiency and yield strength. Therefore, compared to (RGO + CPD) multi-scale synergetic reinforcements, the predicted load transfer strengthening from a single reinforcement is substantially smaller.

In addition to having better mechanical characteristics than Cu (MLM/HEB), RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite has also demonstrated improved conductivity. The exceptional electron transport capabilities of CPD are a result of its unique structure [42]. The CPD clusters are uniformly distributed throughout the copper matrix, allowing them to act as electron transport channels at grain boundaries, relieving overloaded electron transport there, and so encouraging conductivity increase. In particular, the doping of nitrogen atoms in CPD makes it easier for sp2 bonds to form, which lowers the electrical bandgap and promotes carrier transport capacity [43]. Furthermore, a portion of the RGO surface has coupled with the CPD, and the π-π stacking between CPD and RGO can significantly increase the electrical conductivity by optimizing the interfacial charge transfer. In a word, the synergistic reinforcement of (RGO + CPD) has raised the tensile strength and electrical conductivity of Cu matrix simultaneously.

In summary, RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composites were fabricated by a novel powder metallurgy route, both 2D RGO and 0D CPD were highly dispersed within the Cu matrix and obtained strong interface bonding. Due to the existence of robust chemical bonds of (RGO + CPD)-Cu, and the π-π stacking existed between partial RGO and CPD, the introduction of CPD had facilitated the bonding strength between RGO and Cu matrix. The multi-scale synergetic reinforcements were beneficial for the stronger interface bonding, which was critical to improve load transfer efficiency and promote higher yield strength. RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite had the electrical conductivity, tensile strength, and fracture elongation of 93.74% IACS, 367 MPa, and 33.2%, respectively. Compared with pure Cu (prepared by the same process), the synergistic strengthening of (RGO + CPD) in the RGO (MLM)/CPD (HBM)-Cu composite had achieved simultaneously increased mechanical-physical performances. This study provides a new reference for the preparation of high-performance (structure-function integration) Cu matrix composites.

Ruofei Ma: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Yan He: Visualization, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rui Bao: Software, Project administration, Formal analysis. Jianhong Yi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Jingmei Tao: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Caiju Li: Supervision, Software. Xiaonan Mu: Software, Methodology.

Ind. Eng. Chem. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu X, Li X, Li Y, et al. Regulating the Bonding Nature and Location of C-F Bonds in Fluorinated Graphene by Doping Nitrogen Atoms[J]. Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research, 2021, 60(2): 875-884. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.iecr.0c04225

sb:title.sb:maintitle: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy: towards reliable binding energy referencing
Prog. Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Greczynski, L. Hultman. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy: Towards reliable binding energy referencing. Progress in Materials Science, 2020, 107: 100591. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pmatsci.2019.100591

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Compromising science by ignorant instrument calibration-need to revisit half a century of published XPS data
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Greczynski, L. Hultman. Compromising Science by Ignorant Instrument Calibration-Need to Revisit Half a Century of Published XPS Data. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2020, 59:5002-5006. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201916000

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The same chemical state of carbon gives rise to two peaks in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy
Sci. Rep.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Greczynski, L. Hultman. The same chemical state of carbon gives rise to two peaks in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Scientific Reports. 2021,11:11195. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-90780-9

sb:title.sb:maintitle: C1s peak of adventitious carbon aligns to the vacuum level: dire consequences for material's bonding assignment by photoelectron spectroscopy
ChemPhysChem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Greczynski, L. Hultman. C1s Peak of Adventitious Carbon Aligns to the Vacuum Level: Dire Consequences for Material’s Bonding Assignment by Photoelectron Spectroscopy. Chem. Phys. Chem. 2017, 18:1507-1512. https://doi.org/10.1002/cphc.201700126
